content.text: is one of the most fundamental coordination tools in async programming — a shared channel through which concurrent tasks pass data without stepping on each other.
is a coroutine-safe, first-in-first-out (FIFO) data structure that lets multiple async tasks produce and consume items concurrently. It lives in Python's
module and is specifically designed for use within a single event loop — meaning it is

for that). It acts as a buffer between producers (tasks that generate work) and consumers (tasks that process that work), enabling them to run at different speeds without data loss or race conditions.

import asyncio

queue = asyncio.Queue()          # unbounded
queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=10) # bounded: blocks put() when full
There are three queue variants in
— last-in, first-out (like a stack); subclass of Queue
— items dequeued by lowest priority number first; subclass of Queue

In async programs, you often have tasks that naturally work at different rates. A web scraper might download pages quickly but need slow processing. An LLM pipeline might batch-prompt fast but wait on GPU inference. Without a queue, you'd need complex locking, manual coordination, or you'd couple producers tightly to consumers (so one slow consumer blocks the whole pipeline).
) automatically applies backpressure: if the queue is full,
suspends the producer until a consumer frees space. No explicit throttling code needed.
— producers and consumers don't know about each other. You can add or remove workers without touching producer logic.
blocks until every item placed on the queue has been processed and acknowledged with
. This gives a clean, reliable way to know when all work is complete.
-backed set of waiters: coroutines blocked on
A separate set of putters: coroutines blocked on
when the queue is full (bounded only)
and the queue is empty, the event loop suspends your coroutine and parks it in the getter-waiters list. When another coroutine calls
, it wakes the first waiting getter and passes the item directly — no polling, no sleeping.

await queue.put(item)       # add item; suspends if queue is full (bounded)
queue.put_nowait(item)      # add item; raises QueueFull immediately if full
item = await queue.get()    # remove item; suspends if queue is empty
item = queue.get_nowait()   # remove item; raises QueueEmpty immediately if empty
queue.task_done()           # signal that a gotten item has been fully processed
await queue.join()          # suspend until all items have had task_done() called
queue.qsize()               # current number of items
queue.empty()               # True if qsize() == 0
queue.full()                # True if maxsize > 0 and qsize() >= maxsize
(regular functions, raise immediately) is important: use the coroutine versions inside
variants only when you're certain about the queue's state (e.g., in callbacks or when you've already checked
is the right tool whenever coroutines need to hand off work or data to other coroutines running concurrently in the same event loop:
: one producer feeds N workers (e.g., one URL discoverer feeds many page fetchers)
: N producers all feed one consumer that aggregates results
: producers run faster than consumers; the queue absorbs the burst
: output of one processing stage becomes input to the next (scrape → parse → store)
: accumulate individual LLM requests and flush in batches to the model API
You only need to signal an event, not pass data — use
You need one producer to block until
The "queue" has only one item at a time — a simple

import asyncio
import random

async def producer(queue: asyncio.Queue, name: str):
    for i in range(5):
        item = f"{name}-item-{i}"
        await asyncio.sleep(random.uniform(0.1, 0.5))  # simulate work
        await queue.put(item)
        print(f"[{name}] put: {item}  (qsize={queue.qsize()})")

async def consumer(queue: asyncio.Queue, name: str):
    while True:
        item = await queue.get()          # suspends if empty
        print(f"[{name}] got: {item}")
        await asyncio.sleep(0.3)          # simulate processing
        queue.task_done()                 # ← REQUIRED for join() to work

async def main():
    queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=3)      # bounded: at most 3 items buffered

    producers = [
        asyncio.create_task(producer(queue, "P1")),
        asyncio.create_task(producer(queue, "P2")),
    ]
    consumers = [
        asyncio.create_task(consumer(queue, "C1")),
        asyncio.create_task(consumer(queue, "C2")),
        asyncio.create_task(consumer(queue, "C3")),
    ]

    # Wait until all producers are done putting items
    await asyncio.gather(*producers)

    # Wait until all items have been processed
    await queue.join()

    # Cancel idle consumers (they're blocked on get(), waiting forever)
    for c in consumers:
        c.cancel()
    await asyncio.gather(*consumers, return_exceptions=True)

asyncio.run(main())

Chain queues together for staged processing:

async def stage_fetch(input_q: asyncio.Queue, output_q: asyncio.Queue):
    while True:
        url = await input_q.get()
        html = await fetch(url)
        await output_q.put(html)
        input_q.task_done()

async def stage_parse(input_q: asyncio.Queue, output_q: asyncio.Queue):
    while True:
        html = await input_q.get()
        data = parse(html)
        await output_q.put(data)
        input_q.task_done()

async def main():
    url_queue   = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=20)
    html_queue  = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=10)
    data_queue  = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=10)

    tasks = [
        *[asyncio.create_task(stage_fetch(url_queue, html_queue)) for _ in range(5)],
        *[asyncio.create_task(stage_parse(html_queue, data_queue)) for _ in range(3)],
        # ...
    ]
dequeues the lowest-numbered item first. Pass tuples

pq = asyncio.PriorityQueue()
await pq.put((1, "low-priority task"))
await pq.put((0, "urgent task"))

priority, item = await pq.get()  # → (0, "urgent task")
If items are not naturally comparable (arbitrary objects), wrap them:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any

@dataclass(order=True)
class PrioritizedItem:
    priority: int
    item: Any = field(compare=False)

await pq.put(PrioritizedItem(priority=2, item=some_object))
Real-world example: async LLM batch inference server

Consider a FastAPI service that receives individual user prompts, batches them, sends them to an LLM API, and returns results. Without a queue, each request would hit the LLM directly, saturating rate limits immediately.

import asyncio
import uuid
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class InferenceRequest:
    prompt: str
    future: asyncio.Future        # result will be set here

class LLMBatchQueue:
    def __init__(self, max_batch: int = 8, batch_timeout: float = 0.05):
        self._queue: asyncio.Queue[InferenceRequest] = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=256)
        self._max_batch = max_batch
        self._batch_timeout = batch_timeout
        self._worker_task: asyncio.Task | None = None

    async def start(self):
        self._worker_task = asyncio.create_task(self._batch_worker())

    async def infer(self, prompt: str) -> str:
        loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
        future: asyncio.Future[str] = loop.create_future()
        req = InferenceRequest(prompt=prompt, future=future)
        await self._queue.put(req)   # backpressure: suspends if queue is full
        return await future           # caller suspends until result is ready

    async def _batch_worker(self):
        while True:
            # Collect up to max_batch items, wait at most batch_timeout seconds
            batch: list[InferenceRequest] = []
            try:
                # Block until at least one item arrives
                first = await self._queue.get()
                batch.append(first)
                deadline = asyncio.get_event_loop().time() + self._batch_timeout
                while len(batch) < self._max_batch:
                    remaining = deadline - asyncio.get_event_loop().time()
                    if remaining <= 0:
                        break
                    try:
                        item = await asyncio.wait_for(self._queue.get(), timeout=remaining)
                        batch.append(item)
                    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
                        break
            except asyncio.CancelledError:
                break

            # Send the whole batch to the LLM API
            try:
                prompts = [r.prompt for r in batch]
                results = await call_llm_batch_api(prompts)   # single API call
                for req, result in zip(batch, results):
                    req.future.set_result(result)
                    self._queue.task_done()
            except Exception as e:
                for req in batch:
                    req.future.set_exception(e)
                    self._queue.task_done()

    async def stop(self):
        if self._worker_task:
            self._worker_task.cancel()
            await asyncio.gather(self._worker_task, return_exceptions=True)

# WRONG — join() will hang if process() raises
item = await queue.get()
await process(item)
queue.task_done()

# RIGHT — use try/finally
item = await queue.get()
try:
    await process(item)
finally:
    queue.task_done()

to apply backpressure and surface the imbalance early rather than silently exhausting memory.
